planting.note: Direct sow in fall in average to dry, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full to partial sun; wild bergamot cold-stratifies naturally over winter for reliable spring germination and spreads by rhizomes and self-seeding to form loose colonies — avoid rich, consistently moist soils that encourage lax, floppy stems and powdery mildew.

directions: Sow direct in fall in average to dry, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full to partial sun; wild bergamot cold-stratifies naturally over winter and germinates readily in spring without any additional treatment — it is one of the easiest native prairie wildflowers to establish from direct-sown seed. Monarda fistulosa is one of the most widespread and adaptable native wildflowers in North America, ranging from the eastern seaboard to the Rocky Mountains across prairies, open woodlands, roadsides, and meadow edges; from July through September it produces shaggy, two-lipped lavender-pink to pale purple flower heads above aromatic, gray-green foliage that smells strongly of oregano and thyme when crushed — the common name wild bergamot refers to a superficial scent resemblance to bergamot orange, the flavoring in Earl Grey tea. It is an exceptional pollinator plant and is especially important for native mining bees (Andrena spp.) and long-tongued bees; it is also a primary nectar plant for ruby-throated hummingbirds, swallowtail butterflies, and a wide range of native bee species. Wild bergamot spreads slowly by rhizomes to form gradually expanding clumps and self-seeds freely in lean, open conditions; divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor, and site in lean, dry soil to minimize powdery mildew — the species is far more mildew-resistant than the cultivated bee balm Monarda didyma when grown in appropriate dry, well-ventilated conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; spotted beebalm requires excellent drainage and is intolerant of heavy or consistently moist soils — it thrives in the kind of dry, sandy, or rocky ground where most other wildflowers struggle, cold-stratifying naturally over winter for spring germination.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy, gravelly, or rocky soil in full sun; spotted beebalm is strictly a plant of dry, open ground and will not tolerate heavy clay or consistently moist soils — it is one of the finest native wildflowers for hot, dry, infertile conditions where richly blooming plants are difficult to establish. Monarda punctata, also called horsemint, is one of the most botanically unusual native wildflowers in eastern and central North America: its flowers are small, spotted yellow-white with purple-brown dots and held in multiple tiered whorls along the stem, each whorl subtended by showy, leaf-like bracts in soft pink, lavender, or white that persist long after the flowers fade, giving the plant a dramatic, sculptural appearance from July through September that is wholly unlike any other native wildflower. It is one of the very best native plants for small native bees, including many specialist solitary bees that visit Monarda; it is also heavily worked by bumblebees, sweat bees, long-tongued bees, and various wasps, and the aromatic foliage contains high concentrations of thymol, the same antiseptic compound found in garden thyme. Spotted beebalm is a short-lived perennial or biennial that self-sows prolifically in appropriate dry, sandy soils — allow seed heads to ripen and fall in place to maintain a self-sustaining colony, and avoid amending or irrigating the soil, which dramatically shortens the lifespan of individual plants.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; purple prairie clover develops a deep taproot and is extremely difficult to transplant — sow in a permanent location and allow natural cold stratification over winter; scarification or a 24-hour cold-water soak improves germination rates in spring-sown seed.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; purple prairie clover develops a deep, woody taproot in its first season and cannot be successfully transplanted — choose a permanent site, sow seeds in place, and allow them to cold-stratify naturally over winter for reliable spring germination. Dalea purpurea is one of the signature wildflowers of the North American tallgrass and mixed-grass prairie, native across a vast range from Canada south through the Great Plains and into the Gulf Coast states; from June through August it produces distinctive, thimble-like cylindrical flower heads densely packed with tiny, brilliant magenta-purple petals that open progressively from the base of the head upward, creating a moving ring of color and pollen that makes it one of the most recognizable and beloved prairie wildflowers. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that improves soil fertility and is a keystone plant in prairie restorations; the flowers are intensely visited by a wide diversity of native bees including mining bees, sweat bees, small carpenter bees, and specialist Dalea-foraging bees, making it one of the highest-value pollinator plants on the prairie. Purple prairie clover is slow-growing in its first year as it establishes its root system but extremely long-lived and drought-proof once established — individual plants may persist for decades in appropriate lean, dry, well-drained conditions, and the attractive fine-textured foliage remains ornamental from spring through fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; white prairie clover develops a deep taproot that makes transplanting very difficult — sow in a permanent location and rely on natural cold stratification over winter; light scarification or a 24-hour cold-water soak improves germination in spring plantings.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; white prairie clover is essentially the white-flowered counterpart to Dalea purpurea, sharing the same deep-taproot habit, prairie origin, and cultural requirements — it cannot be transplanted once its root system is established, so sow seeds in a permanent location and cold-stratify naturally over winter. Dalea candida is a nitrogen-fixing legume native to the same vast range of tallgrass, mixed-grass, and shortgrass prairies as purple prairie clover, from Manitoba and Saskatchewan south through the Great Plains to Texas and New Mexico; from June through August it produces the same elegant cylindrical flower heads as D. purpurea but in pure white with golden-yellow anthers, giving a cleaner, crisper floral effect that is especially striking when the two species are planted together in a prairie planting or pollinator meadow. The flowers are rich in nectar and pollen and attract a broad range of native bees, including specialist Dalea-foraging bees, mining bees, and many small sweat bees; like purple prairie clover it is a keystone species in prairie ecosystem restorations and is considered a high-value component of any native seed mix for the central United States. White prairie clover is slow to establish but essentially immortal once its deep root system is in place — it is drought-tolerant, fire-adapted, long-lived, and one of the finest native wildflowers available for lean, dry, sunny sites.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; leadplant develops an extraordinarily deep root system — mature specimens have roots documented to 15 feet — making it completely impossible to transplant; sow in a permanent location and allow natural cold stratification and scarification over winter; hot-water scarification significantly improves germination rates when spring-sowing.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, dry to average soil in full sun; leadplant is one of the deepest-rooted plants in the world relative to its above-ground size — individual plants develop root systems reaching 15 feet or more into the soil profile, making it one of the most drought-tolerant and fire-adapted shrubs native to North America, and making any attempt at transplanting futile; it must be established from seed sown in place. Amorpha canescens is a low, spreading prairie shrub with distinctive silver-gray, densely canescent (canescens = gray-woolly) pinnately compound leaves that give the whole plant a soft silver-lavender cast; in late June and July it produces spectacular, dense, upright spikes of tiny, deep purple flowers with bright orange anthers, creating a vivid contrast with the silvery foliage that makes it one of the most ornamentally distinguished native prairie shrubs in North America — the plant's association with the deep, lead-gray soils of undisturbed prairie gives it the common name leadplant. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume, an indicator of high-quality, never-plowed prairie remnants, and an exceptional pollinator plant visited by native bumblebees, sweat bees, and mining bees; leadplant is slower to establish from seed than most forbs, taking 2–3 years to begin flowering, but is then extremely long-lived and essentially maintenance-free, resprouting vigorously from its deep root crown after fire or cutting — fire suppression is one reason it has declined in much of its range, and it responds well to periodic cutting or burning that mimics natural disturbance.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean to moderately fertile, well-drained, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; large-flowered tickseed benefits from natural cold stratification over winter and is one of the few Coreopsis species that performs well in partially shaded woodland edge conditions — avoid heavy, wet, or clay soils.

directions: Sow direct in fall in lean to moderately fertile, well-drained, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; large-flowered tickseed is one of the most shade-tolerant Coreopsis species and is the go-to choice for partially shaded native garden situations where the sun-demanding C. lanceolata and C. tinctoria fail to thrive — it is most at home at woodland edges, open pine woods, and the dappled light of thinned forest understory. Coreopsis major is a native perennial of the southeastern United States, ranging from Virginia south to Florida and west to Tennessee and Alabama, where it grows naturally in dry to average, well-drained, sandy or rocky soils in open pine forests, oak-hickory woodlands, and their sunny edges; its leaves are distinctive — the opposite pairs are so deeply divided they appear to be whorls of six narrow, lance-shaped leaflets, giving the plant a delicate, fine-textured appearance — and from June through August it produces a profusion of bright yellow, 1.5–2 inch daisy flowers with a golden-yellow disk, similar in appearance to other tickseeds but borne on a plant comfortable with dappled light. It is an excellent pollinator plant for native bees, including bumblebees and sweat bees that collect its pollen, and supports several specialist Coreopsis-foraging bees in the Southeast; the seed heads are consumed by small finches and sparrows. Large-flowered tickseed spreads slowly by rhizomes to form modest colonies and self-sows lightly in appropriate conditions, making it a well-behaved addition to native garden plantings in partial shade.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
